title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica
oneLiner: Inflammatory condition of older adults (>50) causing proximal muscle pain/stiffness and high inflammatory markers; responsive to steroids but must exclude giant cell arteritis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA):** Co-exists in 15–20%. Look for new headache (temporal/occipital), jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, visual disturbance (amaurosis fugax, diplopia, sudden vision loss). On exam: tender, thickened, or non-pulsatile temporal artery. Absence of these symptoms does not rule out GCA.
- **Aortic dissection / aneurysm:** Large vessel vasculitis from GCA can cause acute chest/back pain, pulse deficit, syncope, hypotension.
- **Sepsis mimicking PMR:** Fever, hypotension, raised lactate, focal infection. Never assume PMR without checking for infection.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and stiffness (morning >45 min, gelling after rest)
  - Systemic upset: low-grade fever, weight loss, malaise, depression (in up to 50%)
  - Onset subacute (days to weeks)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Pain on active movement of shoulders/hips; limited active ROM (especially external rotation, abduction)
  - No objective muscle weakness (vs myositis)
  - No synovitis or joint swelling (vs RA)
  - Normal muscle tone, reflexes, sensation
  - Temporal artery exam: palpate for tenderness, thickening, loss of pulse (only if GCA suspected)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely -> less likely:
  - **Giant cell arteritis** – same population, may present with headache, jaw claudication, visual symptoms, high ESR/CRP, temporal artery abnormality
  - **Rheumatoid arthritis (RA)** – symmetrical small joint synovitis, anti-CCP+, RF+, morning stiffness shorter (<30 min)
  - **Polymyositis / dermatomyositis** – proximal muscle weakness (not pain/stiffness), high CK, muscle enzymes, EMG changes, myositis antibodies
  - **Fibromyalgia** – chronic widespread pain, normal ESR/CRP, tender points, sleep disturbance, no systemic symptoms
  - **Malignancy** (myeloma, lung, breast) – weight loss, night sweats, lytic lesions, monoclonal spike, anaemia
  - **Osteoarthritis** – joint-specific pain, stiffness <30 min, normal ESR/CRP, X-ray changes (joint space loss, osteophytes)
  - **Infection** (e.g., subacute bacterial endocarditis) – fever, murmur, positive blood cultures, septic emboli
- **Splitter:** Morning stiffness >45 min + high ESR/CRP + no synovitis or weakness = PMR. If temporal artery tenderness → GCA.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Check NEWS2, temperature. Temporal artery exam. Ask specifically about headache, jaw claudication, visual symptoms.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (normocytic anaemia, reactive thrombocytosis)
  - ESR (usually >40 mm/hr), CRP (elevated)
  - LFTs (alkaline phosphatase may be raised)
  - CK (normal – key to rule out myositis)
  - RF / anti-CCP (if suspect RA)
  - Thyroid function, serum protein electrophoresis (if suspect myeloma)
  - Blood cultures (if fever)
- **Imaging:**
  - Not needed for typical PMR
  - Chest X-ray – rule out infection/malignancy
  - Temporal artery ultrasound or MRI if GCA suspected (halo sign, vessel wall thickening)
  - Ultrasound of shoulders/hips – optional; may show subdeltoid bursitis, tenosynovitis
- **Special tests:** Temporal artery biopsy if GCA suspected and imaging equivocal (but start steroids immediately if visual loss).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Bird’s criteria (clinical, useful for primary care): age >65, ESR >40, bilateral shoulder pain, morning stiffness >1 hour, onset <2 weeks, depression/weight loss – need 3 of 6. Also ACR/EULAR 2012 classification criteria (require clinical + ultrasound features).
- **Never-miss test:** Formal GCA assessment – if any red flag (headache, jaw claudication, visual disturbance, temporal artery tenderness), arrange urgent temporal artery imaging or start high-dose steroids.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If GCA suspected:** high-dose prednisolone 40–60mg PO (or IV methylprednisolone 500mg–1g if visual loss) – do not delay for biopsy.
  - **If PMR alone:** start prednisolone 15–20mg PO once daily (morning dose). [Check local policy for exact initial dose; typically 15mg for moderate, 20mg for severe symptoms.]
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor clinical response: significant improvement in pain/stiffness within 3–7 days.
  - Check fasting glucose, BP, bone profile.
  - Start bone protection if predicted steroid duration >3 months:
    - Alendronic acid 70mg PO once weekly (if eGFR >35) + calcium/vitamin D (e.g., Adcal D3 1 tab BD)
    - Consider denosumab if bisphosphonate contraindicated
  - Consider PPI (e.g., omeprazole 20mg daily) if elderly or GI risk.
  - Taper prednisolone after 3–4 weeks once symptoms controlled: reduce by 2.5mg every 2–4 weeks to maintenance of 7.5–10mg, then slower taper.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Rheumatology follow-up (typically 1–2 years of treatment)
  - Monitor ESR/CRP to guide tapering (aim for normal)
  -

rawMarkdown:
## Polymyalgia Rheumatica
**One-liner:** Inflammatory condition of older adults (>50) causing proximal muscle pain/stiffness and high inflammatory markers; responsive to steroids but must exclude giant cell arteritis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA):** Co-exists in 15–20%. Look for new headache (temporal/occipital), jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, visual disturbance (amaurosis fugax, diplopia, sudden vision loss). On exam: tender, thickened, or non-pulsatile temporal artery. Absence of these symptoms does not rule out GCA.
- **Aortic dissection / aneurysm:** Large vessel vasculitis from GCA can cause acute chest/back pain, pulse deficit, syncope, hypotension.
- **Sepsis mimicking PMR:** Fever, hypotension, raised lactate, focal infection. Never assume PMR without checking for infection.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and stiffness (morning >45 min, gelling after rest)
  - Systemic upset: low-grade fever, weight loss, malaise, depression (in up to 50%)
  - Onset subacute (days to weeks)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Pain on active movement of shoulders/hips; limited active ROM (especially external rotation, abduction)
  - No objective muscle weakness (vs myositis)
  - No synovitis or joint swelling (vs RA)
  - Normal muscle tone, reflexes, sensation
  - Temporal artery exam: palpate for tenderness, thickening, loss of pulse (only if GCA suspected)

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely -> less likely:
  - **Giant cell arteritis** – same population, may present with headache, jaw claudication, visual symptoms, high ESR/CRP, temporal artery abnormality
  - **Rheumatoid arthritis (RA)** – symmetrical small joint synovitis, anti-CCP+, RF+, morning stiffness shorter (<30 min)
  - **Polymyositis / dermatomyositis** – proximal muscle weakness (not pain/stiffness), high CK, muscle enzymes, EMG changes, myositis antibodies
  - **Fibromyalgia** – chronic widespread pain, normal ESR/CRP, tender points, sleep disturbance, no systemic symptoms
  - **Malignancy** (myeloma, lung, breast) – weight loss, night sweats, lytic lesions, monoclonal spike, anaemia
  - **Osteoarthritis** – joint-specific pain, stiffness <30 min, normal ESR/CRP, X-ray changes (joint space loss, osteophytes)
  - **Infection** (e.g., subacute bacterial endocarditis) – fever, murmur, positive blood cultures, septic emboli
- **Splitter:** Morning stiffness >45 min + high ESR/CRP + no synovitis or weakness = PMR. If temporal artery tenderness → GCA.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Check NEWS2, temperature. Temporal artery exam. Ask specifically about headache, jaw claudication, visual symptoms.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (normocytic anaemia, reactive thrombocytosis)
  - ESR (usually >40 mm/hr), CRP (elevated)
  - LFTs (alkaline phosphatase may be raised)
  - CK (normal – key to rule out myositis)
  - RF / anti-CCP (if suspect RA)
  - Thyroid function, serum protein electrophoresis (if suspect myeloma)
  - Blood cultures (if fever)
- **Imaging:**
  - Not needed for typical PMR
  - Chest X-ray – rule out infection/malignancy
  - Temporal artery ultrasound or MRI if GCA suspected (halo sign, vessel wall thickening)
  - Ultrasound of shoulders/hips – optional; may show subdeltoid bursitis, tenosynovitis
- **Special tests:** Temporal artery biopsy if GCA suspected and imaging equivocal (but start steroids immediately if visual loss).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Bird’s criteria (clinical, useful for primary care): age >65, ESR >40, bilateral shoulder pain, morning stiffness >1 hour, onset <2 weeks, depression/weight loss – need 3 of 6. Also ACR/EULAR 2012 classification criteria (require clinical + ultrasound features).
- **Never-miss test:** Formal GCA assessment – if any red flag (headache, jaw claudication, visual disturbance, temporal artery tenderness), arrange urgent temporal artery imaging or start high-dose steroids.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If GCA suspected:** high-dose prednisolone 40–60mg PO (or IV methylprednisolone 500mg–1g if visual loss) – do not delay for biopsy.
  - **If PMR alone:** start prednisolone 15–20mg PO once daily (morning dose). [Check local policy for exact initial dose; typically 15mg for moderate, 20mg for severe symptoms.]
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor clinical response: significant improvement in pain/stiffness within 3–7 days.
  - Check fasting glucose, BP, bone profile.
  - Start bone protection if predicted steroid duration >3 months:
    - Alendronic acid 70mg PO once weekly (if eGFR >35) + calcium/vitamin D (e.g., Adcal D3 1 tab BD)
    - Consider denosumab if bisphosphonate contraindicated
  - Consider PPI (e.g., omeprazole 20mg daily) if elderly or GI risk.
  - Taper prednisolone after 3–4 weeks once symptoms controlled: reduce by 2.5mg every 2–4 weeks to maintenance of 7.5–10mg, then slower taper.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Rheumatology follow-up (typically 1–2 years of treatment)
  - Monitor ESR/CRP to guide tapering (aim for normal)
  -
